---
title: "Intervene When Necessary - Not When Bored"
author: "Peter Raidl"
date: "2026-02-24"
image: "Watch_do.png"
---

Coaches regularly introduce fancy exercise variations or new complex programs, even when there is no objective reason to do so. If the old program works well and produces results, why change?

Often, we feel the need to further *optimize*. A program is never perfect, and there is this one particular exercise that would fit so well… Trust me, I feel you!

However, this optimization may just mask the **Bored Specialist**. Our client gets told how to squat maybe once or twice a week, but the coach may teach that exact squat ten times a day. To “spice it up,” we may implement new variations. But for whom? For us or for them?

The key is to **audit ourselves honestly** on three levels:

-   **Is it for the athlete?** There may be physiological (e.g., stagnation or injuries), psychological (e.g., motivation or monotony), or environmental (e.g., equipment availability) reasons to change the plan. If there are not, stick to it!

-   **Is it for my ego?** As coaches, we may feel bored, we want to feel respected and in full control. None of these are reasons for changing the program. But it is still valuable information to you! If you are bored, that is okay. Look at other aspects of your (professional) life you may want to change. Just make sure to check in with your *coaching values* so you don’t confuse your personal needs with your clients' needs.

-   **Is it for job security?** Quite frequently, I hear coaches express their fears of being dispensable. They try to overcome that through innovation and *entertrainment* rather than real coaching. But remember that at the core of good coaching is relationship and trust, not information exchange or entertainment.

Try to answer those questions as honestly as possible. Frequently, not intervening is the best we can do. It means overcoming boredom, insecurity, and pride, and being a coach for the athlete, patient, or client in front of you.
